summary: Spolsky highlights a cognitive bias from Shafir's 1994 research showing that most people stop reasoning through a problem when they hit a disjunction—a point requiring more information—even when that information is irrelevant to the outcome. He connects this to programming, arguing that good programmers overcome this mental block. This ability to push past uncertainty points is especially critical in debugging, where wasting time investigating irrelevant areas is costly. Spolsky suggests this cognitive trait may be one of the key properties distinguishing great programmers from average ones.
key_points: Most people halt their reasoning when they encounter a branching point that seems to require unknown information, even when the outcome is the same regardless
This cognitive limitation, documented in Shafir 1994, reflects an inherent difficulty with disjunctive reasoning
Good programmers overcome this bias, enabling them to efficiently eliminate irrelevant areas during debugging
This capacity for reasoning through uncertainty may be a fundamental trait that separates great programmers from others
money_quotes: Certain types of logical thinking are inherently hard.
Most people will stop working through a problem when they get to a disjunction, that is, a point on the decision tree where you need more information.
This becomes especially interesting when you don't actually need the information because the result would be the same in any case.
There is something that makes most people unwilling to go past uncertainty points in problem solving anyway.
Good programmers usually overcome this problem.
We don't want to waste time investigating areas that could not possibly cause the bug.
This might be one of those interesting brain-properties that distinguishes great programmers from everyone else.
key_insight: Great programmers distinguish themselves by their ability to reason through logical disjunctions—pushing past points of uncertainty when the unknown information wouldn't change the outcome anyway.
